questions.text: Which of the following is the most common timing for uterine perforation to occur during a gynecologic procedure?

answerOptions.text: B. During placement of cervical dilators or hysteroscope

acceptedWrittenAnswers: During placement of cervical dilators or hysteroscope
questions.explanation: Most perforations occur at the start of the procedure due to forceful entry with instruments---especially in the setting of difficult anatomy.
questions.submodule: Richards, E. G., Rehmer, J. M., & Falcone, T. (2023). Perforation during gynecological procedures. JAMA, 329(11), 933–934. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0687

questions.text: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial response when intraoperative uterine perforation is suspected?

answerOptions.text: A. Complete the case quickly and monitor postoperatively
B. Notify anesthesia and nursing teams and assess hemodynamic status
C. Start prophylactic antibiotics and continue the case
D. Immediately begin laparotomy in all patients

acceptedWrittenAnswers: Notify anesthesia and nursing teams and assess hemodynamic status
questions.explanation: The first step is communication and rapid evaluation. Stabilization takes priority, especially if the patient is hemodynamically unstable.
questions.submodule: Richards, E. G., Rehmer, J. M., & Falcone, T. (2023). Perforation during gynecological procedures. JAMA, 329(11), 933–934. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0687

questions.text: Which uterine perforation site is most associated with risk of broad ligament hematoma due to proximity to uterine vessels?
questions.explanation: Lateral perforations are concerning due to proximity to uterine arteries and the broad ligament, increasing the risk of concealed hemorrhage.
questions.submodule: Richards, E. G., Rehmer, J. M., & Falcone, T. (2023). Perforation during gynecological procedures. JAMA, 329(11), 933–934. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0687

questions.text: A 42-year-old patient has a lateral uterine perforation during hysteroscopic resection with active bleeding. Despite laparoscopic sponge compression, a broad ligament hematoma continues to expand. What is the next best step?

answerOptions.text: A. Close the procedure and observe postoperatively
C. Open the space, identify, and ligate bleeding vessels

acceptedWrittenAnswers: Open the space, identify, and ligate bleeding vessels
questions.explanation: Persistent hematoma expansion despite conservative measures necessitates surgical exploration and vessel ligation.
questions.submodule: Richards, E. G., Rehmer, J. M., & Falcone, T. (2023). Perforation during gynecological procedures. JAMA, 329(11), 933–934. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0687

questions.text: Which of the following statements about long-term consequences of uterine perforation is true?

answerOptions.text: A. All uterine perforations increase the risk of uterine rupture in future pregnancy
B. Cesarean delivery is required for all patients with prior uterine perforation
C. Uterine adhesions and infertility can occur as long-term sequelae
D. Perforations that occur during initial cervical dilation require laparotomy

acceptedWrittenAnswers: Uterine adhesions and infertility can occur as long-term sequelae
questions.explanation: Perforation may lead to intrauterine adhesions or infertility. However, isolated cervical dilation-related perforation does not mandate cesarean delivery or laparotomy unless other risk factors exist.
questions.submodule: Richards, E. G., Rehmer, J. M., & Falcone, T. (2023). Perforation during gynecological procedures. JAMA, 329(11), 933–934. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0687

questions.text: Which of the following is considered a preventive strategy for uterine perforation during gynecologic procedures?

answerOptions.text: A. Avoiding use of a tenaculum
C. Performing a bimanual exam prior to dilation
D. Using monopolar energy in the fundus

acceptedWrittenAnswers: Performing a bimanual exam prior to dilation
questions.explanation: This helps assess uterine position and minimize misalignment during instrument entry.
questions.submodule: Richards, E. G., Rehmer, J. M., & Falcone, T. (2023). Perforation during gynecological procedures. JAMA, 329(11), 933–934. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0687

questions.text: What is a common risk factor that increases the chance of uterine perforation during hysteroscopy?
questions.explanation: Thinner, more fragile uterine walls in postmenopausal women increase perforation risk.
questions.submodule: Richards, E. G., Rehmer, J. M., & Falcone, T. (2023). Perforation during gynecological procedures. JAMA, 329(11), 933–934. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0687

questions.text: Which imaging modality is the first-line evaluation for suspected postoperative retroperitoneal hematoma?

answerOptions.text: A. CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast

questions.explanation:
CT with intravenous contrast is the gold standard imaging modality for hemodynamically stable patients with suspected intra-abdominal or retroperitoneal hemorrhage, with sensitivity and specificity approaching 96–100%. MRI can accurately detect hematomas; however, its longer acquisition time makes it less suitable for initial evaluation in acute settings compared to CT. Ultrasound has a limited role in assessing retroperitoneal hematomas.

Ultrasound is, however, the first-line imaging modality for suspected broad ligament hematoma, particularly in the postpartum period when these hematomas most commonly occur. An extended focused assessment with sonography for trauma (E-FAST) is also a rapid and useful tool for detecting intra-abdominal free fluid during initial trauma evaluation, though it has significant limitations in identifying retroperitoneal pathology.
questions.submodule:
Richards, E. G., Rehmer, J. M., & Falcone, T. (2023). Perforation during gynecological procedures. JAMA, 329(11), 933–934. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0687

Verma, Nupur et al. ACR Appropriateness Criteria® Suspected Retroperitoneal Bleed. Journal of the American College of Radiology, Volume 18, Issue 11, S482 - S487

questions.text: Which of the following intraoperative findings should raise suspicion for occult uterine perforation, even in the absence of visible organ injury?
questions.explanation: Hematuria during hysteroscopy is a red flag for possible bladder or uterine wall injury.
questions.submodule: Richards, E. G., Rehmer, J. M., & Falcone, T. (2023). Perforation during gynecological procedures. JAMA, 329(11), 933–934. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0687

questions.text: What is the recommended approach for an anterior uterine perforation during hysteroscopy when bladder involvement is suspected?
questions.explanation: Anterior/posterior perforations require cystoscopy or proctoscopy depending on suspected organ injury.
questions.submodule: Richards, E. G., Rehmer, J. M., & Falcone, T. (2023). Perforation during gynecological procedures. JAMA, 329(11), 933–934. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0687

questions.text: Which statement best describes fundal perforation caused by a dilator or hysteroscope without active bleeding?

answerOptions.text: D. Requires thermal coagulation of the defect

questions.explanation: If no energy device or morcellator was involved and no active bleeding is seen.
questions.submodule: Richards, E. G., Rehmer, J. M., & Falcone, T. (2023). Perforation during gynecological procedures. JAMA, 329(11), 933–934. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0687

questions.text: What clinical symptom may signal delayed recognition of a complex uterine perforation postoperatively?
questions.explanation: Delayed signs may include infection, bleeding, or abscess formation.
questions.submodule: Richards, E. G., Rehmer, J. M., & Falcone, T. (2023). Perforation during gynecological procedures. JAMA, 329(11), 933–934. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0687

questions.text: A patient develops severe lower abdominal pain and fever 4 days after a uterine procedure. CT scan shows a pelvic abscess. What is the most likely cause in the context of uterine perforation?

answerOptions.text: B. Iatrogenic infection from bowel injury

questions.explanation: Undiagnosed bowel injuries can lead to pelvic abscesses and systemic infection.
questions.submodule: Richards, E. G., Rehmer, J. M., & Falcone, T. (2023). Perforation during gynecological procedures. JAMA, 329(11), 933–934. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0687

questions.text: Which of the following would not be an appropriate part of the management plan for a suspected bladder injury during uterine perforation?

answerOptions.text: C. Empirical treatment with ciprofloxacin only

questions.explanation: Ciprofloxacin alone is not adequate. Broad-spectrum antibiotics should be used, and a urology consult is standard.
questions.submodule: Richards, E. G., Rehmer, J. M., & Falcone, T. (2023). Perforation during gynecological procedures. JAMA, 329(11), 933–934. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0687

questions.text: Which patient is at lowest risk for uterine perforation during hysteroscopy?

answerOptions.text: B. Postmenopausal woman with vaginal atrophy
C. Premenopausal patient undergoing diagnostic hysteroscopy
D. Patient with history of myomectomy

questions.explanation: Diagnostic procedures in premenopausal patients have the lowest documented perforation rates (0.1-0.5%).
questions.submodule: Richards, E. G., Rehmer, J. M., & Falcone, T. (2023). Perforation during gynecological procedures. JAMA, 329(11), 933–934. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0687
